snapshot.scope: Independent review of the large-paste Agent-turn boundary: canonical Unicode and UTF-8 limits, side-effect-free ordered staging, atomic exact submission, exact-once managed-source preservation, reference-only history, safe UI projection, retry identity, and unchanged status.

reviewers.task: Large pasted content boundary contract review
reviewers.independence: Product Planning reviews only contracts and executable governance; Development still owns every implementation deletion, rewrite, security test, package and runtime artifact.

dimensions.summary: One composer boundary distinguishes ordinary paste, staged large paste and independent immediate file drop without introducing another product mode.
UI, IPC, source, data and Job owners share one manifest for exact text, ordered staging, preservation and retry semantics.
PIGE-CAP-002 and E2.02 remain planned while acceptance names the exact executable evidence still required.
The manifest and mutation gate give UI, preload and service owners one constants source and fail deterministic drift.
Numeric limits are defined once in a compact manifest and prose owners project only their own observable or durability boundary.

verification.evidence: Canonical values, owner markers, planned statuses and deterministic mutation controls pass.
Canonical Job reliability plus submitted-turn, high-risk and Provider-send contracts remain single-owned and fail safe.
The full merge-candidate governance suite passes after trace, semantic and reviewed-snapshot regeneration.

coordination.compatibility: The current 8,000-code-point request bound stays authoritative while staged large-paste implementation is pending; no capture-only route is restored.
coordination.acknowledgement: Product Planning freezes one cross-layer manifest; Development and UI bind it in one later candidate without Requirement, Exit or Phase promotion.

nonBlockingRisks: The manifest is frozen but staged large-paste runtime and UI binding remain unimplemented.
Windows and Linux qualification remains deferred and unclaimed during early macOS-first development.
